title: Prompts Drift Like Code
concept: Why prompts need version control, changelogs, and rollback just like application source code.

exercises.prompt: Quick check: when a software developer changes a line of code and it breaks the app, what do they usually do?

options.text: Look at the code's version history and revert the bad change
Delete the entire app and start over
Just leave it broken and hope no one notices

exercises.explain: Developers rely on version history to find exactly what changed and undo it. This same habit — tracking changes and being able to revert them — is exactly what we need for AI prompts too, which is what this lesson is all about.

cards.title: One Tiny Word Change...
cards.body: A team changed one sentence in their AI assistant's prompt to 'be more helpful.' The next day, it started giving unsafe advice to customers. Nobody remembered what the prompt looked like before.

cards.title: Prompt Versioning
cards.body: Prompt versioning means saving every version of a prompt, tracking what changed and why, and being able to go back to an older version if a new one breaks something. It treats prompts like code files, not throwaway text.

cards.title: A Prompt Is Like a Recipe
cards.body: If you tweak a recipe without writing down what changed, you'll forget why the cake stopped rising. Prompts are the same — every small edit changes the 'taste' of the AI's answers, so you need a written history.

cards.title: A Real Slip-Up
cards.body: A team edits their chatbot prompt to 'sound more casual.' Suddenly it starts giving unsafe medical advice. Without version history, they can't tell which line caused it or restore the safe version quickly — they have to guess and re-test everything.

cards.title: Myth: Prompts Are 'Just Text'
cards.body: Myth: since prompts are just words, they don't need the same care as code. Reality: a single word change in a prompt can flip an AI's behavior, tone, or safety — just like a single line of code can crash an app. Prompts deserve the same tracking.

cards.title: Tracked vs. Untracked Prompts
cards.body: Compare a team that treats prompts like disposable notes versus one that treats them like code files with history.

left.text: Edited directly, no history, no idea what changed — if it breaks, nobody knows why or how to fix it fast.

right.text: Each edit is saved with a note on what changed and why — if it breaks, you can compare versions and roll back instantly.

exercises.prompt: Why do prompts need version control just like source code?

options.text: Because prompts are written in a special programming language
Because small edits to prompts can significantly change AI behavior, and teams need to track, compare, and revert those changes
Because AI models require prompts to be stored in code repositories to run
Because version control makes prompts run faster

exercises.explain: Prompts are plain language, not code syntax, but they behave like code in one key way: small changes can cause big, sometimes unexpected shifts in AI output. Version control lets teams track what changed, when, and why — and undo changes that go wrong.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Once a prompt seems to work well, it never needs to be tracked or updated again.
exercises.explain: False. Prompts 'drift' as models update, user needs change, and team members tweak wording over time. Without ongoing tracking, a prompt that worked great last month could quietly degrade or break without anyone noticing why.

exercises.sentence: If a new prompt causes bad outputs, teams need the ability to rollback to the last working version.
exercises.explain: Rollback means reverting to a previous, known-good version of the prompt — the same safety net that code version control gives developers when a new release breaks something.

exercises.prompt: Which of these changelog entries looks AI-generated (auto-filled with no real detail) rather than written by a thoughtful human engineer?

options.text: "Updated the changelog entry accordingly to reflect the update made to this version of the prompt."
"v2.3: Removed the 'always apologize first' line — it was making refund responses sound insincere."
"v1.4: Added explicit instruction to cite sources, after users reported unverifiable claims."

exercises.explain: The first entry is circular and empty — it says a change was made without explaining what changed or why, a common sign of vague auto-generated filler text. Good changelogs (like the other two) name the exact edit and the real-world reason behind it.

exercises.prompt: Your team's support-bot prompt was edited 12 times this month by 3 different people, and now it sometimes gives rude answers. What's the BEST fix going forward?

options.text: Ban anyone from editing the prompt again
Put the prompt in version control with changelogs, so you can review changes and roll back the exact edit that caused it
Rewrite the whole prompt from scratch each week
Add more instructions to the prompt telling it to 'be nice'

exercises.explain: When many people edit a prompt over time, you need a paper trail. Version control with changelogs lets you see exactly which edit changed the tone, and rollback lets you instantly restore the last good version instead of guessing or rewriting from scratch. Banning edits or vaguely adding 'be nice' doesn't fix the root problem: untracked changes.
